attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man with a history of poorly controlled diabetes presents to the clinic with a non-healing ulcer on his right foot. The ulcer developed a few weeks ago and has been progressively getting worse. On examination, there is an open ulcer with surrounding erythema and oedema on the plantar aspect of the right foot. The patient has diminished sensation in his lower extremities, and there is no evidence of cellulitis or abscess. There are no signs of systemic infection. Laboratory results shows:

  • White blood cell count: 15.0 x10^9/L (4-11)

  • Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 25 mm/hour (0-15)

X-ray imaging reveals bone destruction in the affected area.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause?
attributes.explanation:
In patients with poorly controlled diabetes, foot ulcers are a common complication that can lead to osteomyelitis. Staphylococcus aureus, Anaerobes like Bacteroides and Gram negative organisms are common pathogens which in combination are associated with serious diabetic foot infections, including osteomyelitis. That is why the first line antibiotic treatment is with combination therapy, ie Broad spectrum antibiotic (like first generation Cephalosporin) combined with anti Anaerobe until the results of microbiology are available.

MRSA is a gram-positive bacterium that is resistant to multiple antibiotics, including methicillin. Patients with diabetes are at increased risk for MRSA infections due to impaired immune function, poor wound healing, and frequent exposure to healthcare settings. It is a common pathogen associated with diabetic foot infections and osteomyelitis. It is a particularly concerning pathogen due to its ability to cause severe and difficult-to-treat infections.

Streptococcus pyogenes, also known as Group A Streptococcus, is a gram-positive bacterium that can cause various infections, including skin and soft tissue infections. While it is a common cause of cellulitis, it is less frequently associated with diabetic foot infections and osteomyelitis compared to MRSA.

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a gram-negative bacterium that is known for its ability to cause opportunistic infections, particularly in immunocompromised individuals. While it can cause infections in diabetic foot ulcers, it is more commonly associated with infections in burn wounds and chronic wounds that are exposed to water or soil. In the given case, there is no mention of exposure to such environments, making Pseudomonas aeruginosa less likely.

Bacteroides fragilis is an anaerobic gram-negative bacterium that is part of the normal flora in the gastrointestinal tract. It is more commonly associated with intra-abdominal infections, such as appendicitis or diverticulitis, however in diabetics it can be part of combination of pathogens causing significant foot infection.

Escherichia coli is a gram-negative bacterium and a common cause of urinary tract infections and gastrointestinal infections. However it can occasionally be associated with diabetic foot infections, especially in combination with the previously mentioned organisms.
learningPoints: Osteomyelitis is a well recognised complication of diabetic foot infection, being present  in approximately 10%-15% of moderate and in 50% of severe infections.
references: Giurato L, Meloni M, Izzo V, Uccioli L. Osteomyelitis in diabetic foot: A comprehensive overview. World J Diabetes. 2017 Apr 15;8(4):135-142. doi: 10.4239/wjd.v8.i4.135. PMID: 28465790; PMCID: PMC5394733.
